data.title: General Motors: Not The Time For A Premium
data.description:
Investment Thesis

General Motors (NYSE:GM) has enjoyed strong bullish momentum over the last twelve months, but more precisely since April of that year, when tariffs hit U.S. equities hard at first glance, but subsequently, with the impacts better digested, triggered a lower risk perception, and the stock rallied further.

Although the Detroit-based automaker has shown itself to be quite capable of mitigating the impact of tariffs by around 30% with capacity realignment measures, the company is still promising guidance well below what was initially expected for 2025, with analysts being forced to revise their spreadsheets downwards.

However, as a rule of thumb, downward revisions suggest price pressure (at least in the short to medium term), and surprisingly, what happened was the opposite. GM continued to expand multiples with a market apparently not accounting for the impacts on the company's bottom line, even after the partial mitigation suggested.

And now, I believe GM's bullish thesis tends to be primarily driven by evidence of EBIT-adjusted margins recovering towards double digits, with 3Q25 (scheduled in October 21) being the first litmus test for this.

As for stocks that trade at a premium (even if in GM's case it's a premium vs. its own historical average), arguably it's necessary to deliver or better yet, overdeliver. I don't believe this will be GM's pattern in 3Q25 and potentially in 2H25, which, in my view, contradicts the company trading at a premium valuation at this very moment.

Therefore, I think GM deserves a Hold rating for now, waiting on the sidelines for better indications of margins close to pre-tariff levels.

From Double-Digit Margins to Downward Revisions

In recent years, especially between 2020 and 2023, GM has been delivering low-double-digit operating margins (EBIT) in the North America region, which is the company's profit-generating core. In fact, CEO Mary Barra and the management team have repeatedly communicated the goal of maintaining double-digit margins in the region, while accelerating the launch of EVs (Chevy Blazer EV, Equinox EV, Cadillac Lyriq).

However, the latest 2Q25 results pointed to a timid EBIT-adjusted margin coming in at 6.1% in North America, well below the ~10% envisioned, largely pressured by tariff headwinds ($1.1 billion impact).

Overall, EBIT-adjusted margins came in at 6.4%—it's important to note that "adjusted" here equates to the GAAP measure of net income attributable to stockholders, and therefore may vary from traditional EBIT margin metrics reported on platforms such as Seeking Alpha itself.

That said, in a way, it is no longer automatic to assume margins "above 10%" in the current environment—even though GM has already demonstrated that such levels are plausible in more stable conditions. So, in my interpretation, the main driver in GM's bullish thesis tends to be much more about stabilization and the ability to restore or protect margins as the transition to EVs progresses.

One point of note here is when we put side by side the difference in the price at which GM shares are priced and its EBIT margin (ttm) over the last three years, we can see that the spread between the two is at the highest historical level in the series, which in theory should reflect high market optimism (potentially with GM stock taking off from operating fundamentals) not necessarily consistent with an EBIT-adjusted margin scenario under pressure.

This implies that, in the current environment, investors need to be aware that a significant part of GM's positive momentum reflects future expectations, but that, curiously, these expectations have been sharply revised downward, which in most cases would have dampened the bullish momentum.

For example, when we look at analysts' expectations for 3Q25 and 4Q25, in the last six months, EPS of $2.87 and $2.37 were anticipated, while now $2.34 and $1.89 are anticipated—which means a reduction in expectations of 22.5% and 25.4%, respectively.

Even though the recent figures reported in Q2 2025 showed resilience in adjusted EBIT margins above expectations, given the severity of the impact of tariffs, the downward revision of EPS to less than 1% in 2025 and 2.6% in 2026 over the last three months is still modest compared to the previous outlook for GM's bottom line for 2025 posted in 4Q24.

Looking Ahead to Q3

It seems to me that the market is very optimistic that adjusted EBIT margins could return to levels of 8-10%, mainly due to the guidance provided by the CFO, indicating that in H2/25 EBIT will be ~$1.75 billion lower than in H1.

This is also because tariffs account for the vast majority of the decline in margins, much more than demand or pricing issues, with the market remaining solid (SAAR ~16.6 million), prices stable, and GM gaining market share.

However, Q3 is expected to be weaker than Q2 because (1) it will be another quarter full of tariffs; (2) lower wholesale seasonality; and (3) higher spending on new generation trucks and capacity expansion in the U.S.

Therefore, EBIT-adjusted margins will likely remain in the 6-7% range until tariff mitigation measures begin to take effect (new investments, 30% offset cited by CEO Barra, and cost discipline).

Another key secondary point, which I believe will be a topic of discussion on earnings day, is the focus on capital allocation, EV profitability, and the adoption curve.

Over the past three years, GM has reduced its outstanding shares by 4%, 17%, and 13.8%, respectively, spending a total of $20.6 billion between 2022 and 2024 on buybacks—$7.73 billion in the last twelve months.

In 2Q25, the management team reiterated open market repurchases resumed in July with $4.3 billion of remaining authorization, showing a commitment to returning capital when margins and cash flow allow—although in 2Q25 no shares were repurchased for the first time in several quarters.

GM's CFO emphasized that capital should be allocated in the coming quarters where it can structurally improve cost and performance. In other words, I anticipate that buybacks will take a prolonged pause while strategically prioritizing measures to enhance margins, while ramping up EVs in a sustainable manner.

Already making a link with EVs, I believe it will be crucial to monitor EV unit margins. In the case of the Chevrolet Equinox EV, in 2Q25, the vehicle gained ~6 points of market share in the segment (ICE+EV) year-over-year, showing strong market acceptance. GM already ranks Chevrolet as #2 in EVs in the U.S. and Cadillac as #5 overall.

In the case of Super Cruise, GM reported an expectation of $200 million from the vehicle in 2025 and doubling in 2026, with $4 billion in deferred software revenue. Advances in this line in 3Q25 towards these goals tend to be a very welcome sign.

Last but not least, in 2Q25, inventory and EV pricing adjustments (in this case, $600 million in write-downs) were temporary and should improve as production and demand stabilize. And that is precisely what needs to be seen in 3Q25, not the opposite.

On Valuations and Final Remarks

When we look at GM's forward EV/EBITDA and forward P/E multiples, it is quite clear that since 2025, there has been a clear re-rating on the stock multiples, as it has since traded at levels above its historical average over the last five years. In terms of domestic comparison, we can see that Ford Motors (F) has followed a similar trajectory.

As I see it, this re-rating (which took a brief pause in April due to tariffs) reflects a combination of successful strategies converging on GM delivering $14.9 billion in EBIT-adj. in 2024, close to the high-end of the guidance ($15 billion). And even though initially for 2025 an EBIT-adj. of $13.7-$15.7 billion was estimated (later readjusted due to tariffs to $10-12.5 billion).

But as GM reiterates its guidance (albeit lower than at the beginning of the year) in 2Q25, and as tariffs headwinds are likely to diminish, the thesis is proving to be more de-risked, paving the way for continued bullish sentiment and a seemingly new pattern of multiple established for U.S. legacy car makers, rather than any irrational valuation increase.

From my perspective, what’s happening with GM is that the market sees the tariff headwinds as less bad than expected, which is driving multiples higher—but it’s still a bad situation, and it’s going to keep the company from getting back to the double-digit EBIT-adjusted margins it worked hard to achieve over the past few years.

And because the company is at a stage of needing to "prove" to the markets that it can recover or stabilize operating margins, a premium valuation shouldn't make much sense at the moment, which leads me to believe that a correction is quite likely in the short to medium term.

data.title: Alphabet's Next Trillion-Dollar Opportunity Could Be AI
data.description:
Not long ago, I wrote about Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG), and the analysis “Betting Against Alphabet? You'll Need A Better Bear Case” has aged well (you can check it here), with a total return of 27% in just over two months.

The DoJ's positive resolution was just one of the bright spots for the company, which managed to regain market confidence and reposition itself as a leader in AI and a company with a lot of room to grow.

Obviously, there are still uncertainties surrounding the thesis, and for that reason, the easy money seems to have already been made. But in my view, the recent increase in valuation is not enough to be pessimistic about Google, and in fact, it is possible to envision scenarios where the gains are even greater.

Google’s Core Could Easily Worth $2 trillion

In other articles, I have already mentioned that YouTube alone at Alphabet is likely to be worth more than $400 billion. YouTube's advertising business alone could easily be worth $275 billion if we consider a price-to-sales ratio of 8x.

The search business, despite its uncertain prospects, is worth more than $970 billion if the price-to-sales ratio is 5x, less than half the multiple of Meta (META).

Google Cloud is a third part that is central to the AI and infrastructure thesis. GCP (Google Cloud Platform) already has more than $1.1 billion in operating income per quarter and continues to grow and gain margin rapidly.

Recently, in addition to outperforming peers such as Amazon's AWS (AMZN), which is a good sign, the company also demonstrated in a presentation (as in the image below) that GCP manages to be the best performance and cost for AI infrastructure, and has already accumulated several important customers such as Salesforce (CRM), Walmart (WMT), Ford (F), Anthropic, and so on. There are also several AI model customers, from McDonald's (MCD) to Adobe (ADBE).

Increasingly, the company is able to demonstrate that it can compete head-to-head with players such as Azure and AWS, which have more scale and some additional competitive advantages, such as the partnership with Microsoft's (MSFT) OpenAI. And to be honest, we, Google shareholders, don't need the company to surpass these two for value to continue to be generated. As long as it continues to perform well in this market, which is expected to grow at a CAGR of 20% in the coming years, there is already a lot of value to be unlocked.

If we estimate that the margin will remain static going forward, then the annual operating income of $4.4 billion will become $10.9 billion in 2030, growing ‘only’ 20% per year, i.e., keeping pace with market growth, without considering efficiency gains and market share. What is a fair value for one of the world's leading cloud companies? 20x operating income? 25x? These still seem like very conservative numbers, but using 20x, we already have more than $200 billion for this part as well.

Above, even by considering low cloud growth, a very conservative multiple for Search and YouTube Ads, we already justify half of Alphabet's market cap, and with a lot of potential for positive surprises such as margin improvement, continued search growth for a few years, etc.

In addition, we have Google Network, Google Subscriptions, and Other Bets, which generate $17 billion in revenue per quarter.

Subscriptions, being very reliable and predictable, as well as a segment that should continue to grow significantly with YouTube Premium, Google Drive, and other tools, could also see something like a 55% operating margin and a fair multiple of 30x, which would give us a fair price of over $600 billion for Google.

In other words, disregarding much of the cloud's potential and being very conservative with the search multiple, it is still possible to justify a market cap of over $2 trillion for Google. Being more optimistic with the above assumptions, it is easy to reach $2.5 trillion or a little more.

But let's remain conservative and estimate that AI alone could be worth almost $1 trillion in market cap for Google.

AI Could Add About $1 Trillion Over the Next Few Decades

Before delving deeper into assumptions, we must try to list the ways in which Google can monetize AI. With LLMs alone, it is possible to think of a few methods. The main one would be charging users a subscription fee, but we also have take-rate on sales. Other ways, such as API, enterprise licensing, advertising, and other types of services, such as consulting, may also eventually be more relevant to AI in the coming years.

There are concrete reasons to be optimistic about AI. According to PWC, GDP could increase by more than $15 trillion by 2030 due to economic gains from AI, which should be mainly affected by labor productivity.

The artificial intelligence market size could reach $3.5 trillion in 2033. And although the market is far from being just LLM, we could use Gemini's market share, which is about 13.4%, as a proxy. In particular, I believe that with the consolidation of this market, Google's market share could increase, but let's not be too optimistic.

Again, in this scenario (proxy), Google's AI market size in 2033 could be $470 billion. Almost half a trillion just for being a relevant player in the AI industry.

To see if this makes any sense or if it's really too much, let's make bottom-up assumptions.

Currently, Gemini has about 400 million MAUs, and that's with rapid growth. Let's assume about 50 million per year increase in that number, and thus, the estimate is 800 million in 2033.

The question now is, how many of these users will be paying users? This is a difficult assumption, not least because it is a very immature market, and it will depend on which model Google decides to follow, whether something more dedicated to subscriptions, something more freemium like Spotify (SPOT), or some less balanced model more focused on take-rates and advertising.

If 20% of this base is premium subscribers, we would have 160 million subscribers. With an average ticket of $35 per month (remember, this is 8 years from now), annual revenue would be $67 billion. I know that 160 million paying subscribers may seem like a lot at first glance. But Spotify had almost 700 million MAUs in Q2, and of those, 40% are premium subscribers, and even though we are talking about a very consolidated and market-leading company, Gemini is in a niche that encompasses entertainment, leisure, but mainly productivity.

Okay, $67 billion in revenue. But we would still have over 600 million users who use the platform for free and could be monetized in other ways.

A good example is that Booking Holdings (BKNG) has gross bookings of about $180 billion per year, and its take rate is 14%. Considering that many people already use LLMs to research the best laptop to buy, there is room to monetize these gross bookings. The travel market is also very interesting because, with just accommodation and flights, gross bookings could easily exceed $1,000 per user.

To avoid being overly optimistic with these assumptions, I will estimate an average of $500 per user per year, and with a take-rate of 10%, Google could generate an additional $40 billion in revenue with this model alone in 2033.

I will disregard advertising because monetization through take-rate is already high.

Note that in this scenario, we are already looking at more than $100 billion in revenue. It may seem like an unrealistic estimate, but it is not that far-fetched. The chart below from Future Search shows that in a “best case scenario,” OpenAI revenue could reach $100 billion by 2027, and we are talking about Google's revenue in 2033.

Another point to consider is that most of Google's AI revenue should also come from enterprise, with higher ticket prices for companies and also in APIs.

Even if this other part of Google's AI revenue were 50% of consumer revenue (a number I picked arbitrarily), we would be talking about $150 billion in revenue. A 30% margin is an income of $45 billion. A fair multiple of 20x gives us another $900 billion in market cap for Google just for AI.

Putting the Pieces Together

Putting it all together, we are talking about roughly $2 trillion in market cap for Alphabet's core business and another $900 billion in AI optionalities over the next few years. The current market cap is $2.96 trillion. Therefore, one could argue that Google's margin of safety is not that good, mainly because the assumptions for AI in 2033 are extremely uncertain and volatile, serving more as an exercise to see what the potential would be if the company performed very well and still had a relevant share of that market.

But I disagree that Google's margin of safety is tight. The main reason is that the $2 trillion core we estimated in the first section was very conservative. Just a better growth assumption for GCP along with a re-rating of Search could unlock a few hundred billion more for Alphabet's core market cap. Another point is that in this calculation, we completely disregarded Network revenue, which is still around $28 billion per year. We also did not consider Waymo in this fair market cap, which in a few years could also be worth a few hundred billion. In other words, it is not just AI that is a relevant optionality; there is a huge addressable market for Alphabet, and positive surprises could happen in the coming decades to the point of unlocking hundreds of billions more in revenue and shareholder value.

And moving on to a more traditional valuation, does a company that can add so much to both its revenue and its TAM in the coming decades, with a strong presence in the world's fastest-growing industries (digital ads, AI, and cloud), deserve to be traded at a forward P/E of 24x?

Even maintaining these compressed multiples such as 8x sales, if the company achieves something close to $700 billion in revenue in 2030, which does not seem impossible to me, given the growth of its core businesses (Search, YouTube, and Cloud) combined with several moonshots, we are already talking about a market cap of $5.6 trillion, or an upside of 93% in 5 years (total return of ~14% per year).

The Bottom Line

That said, I believe that even though the “easy money” has already been made, Alphabet’s potential has not diminished. In fact, with less risk related to divestments from its ecosystem and the consolidation of AI, it is possible to envision scenarios where the company can create significant value for shareholders.

Therefore, my rating remains a BUY for GOOGL stock.

data.title: HighPeak Energy: A Look At Its Challenges At $60 WTI Oil
data.description:
HighPeak Energy (NASDAQ:HPK) announced the retirement of founder Jack Hightower, who was its CEO and Board Chairman as well as essentially the majority owner of the company (through the HighPeak Funds).

Hightower is being replaced as CEO by Michael Hollis (at least on an interim basis), and a committee (including Hollis) will manage the HighPeak Funds. Hollis has been President of HighPeak Energy for five years, so there is a lot of continuity there.

HighPeak is in a challenging situation at $60 WTI oil, though. If oil stays around that level, HighPeak's liquidity will start to decline late in 2026 when it is required to start making its $30 million quarterly term loan repayments again.

HighPeak should be fine in a $70 WTI oil scenario, but at $60 WTI oil, I believe that HighPeak will basically be at breakeven free cash flow after paying its dividend (and before any term loan repayments). This assumes that it attempts to maintain oil production levels.

I am slightly trimming my estimate of HighPeak's value by $0.25 per share to $8.25 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil scenario. While HighPeak has some upside in that pricing scenario, I am remaining neutral on it due to the risks if oil remains more than slightly below $70 into 2027.

Management Changes

HighPeak announced that Jack Hightower has retired from his role as CEO and Board Chairman of the company. Michael Hollis (HighPeak's current President) has been appointed as interim CEO, while the company has mentioned that it is searching for a permanent CEO.

Although no exact reason has been given for the timing of Hightower's retirement (beyond needing time to focus on health and personal endeavors), a transition makes sense given that Hightower is 77 years old.

Hollis has plenty of experience, having served as President and COO of Diamondback Energy before joining HighPeak. Hollis joined HighPeak/Pure Acquisition Corp just after the first business combination attempt was announced in late 2019.

Jack Hightower is also retiring from managing the HighPeak Funds, which are a set of companies that own approximately 64.4% of HighPeak Energy's common stock. A committee comprised of Michael Hollis, Daniel Silver (HighPeak's VP of Finance) and Ryan Hightower (Jack's son and HighPeak's VP of Business Development) will manage the HighPeak funds.

Jack Hightower is receiving a cash separation payment of $2.4 million, along with having 1,385,500 unvested shares fully vest. He is forfeiting the right to exercise various outstanding stock options.

Potential 2026 Outlook

I have assumed that HighPeak can average 32,000 barrels per day of oil production in 2026, with total production averaging 48,000 BOEPD. This would be fairly close to my expectations around its 2H 2025 production, with oil production down 1% and total production up 2% as HighPeak's production gets a bit gassier.

The current strip for 2026 is around $60 WTI oil, and at that oil price, HighPeak may be able to generate $739 million in revenues after hedges.

HighPeak has around 40% of its 2026 oil production (based on this scenario) hedged, although most of those hedges have a floor at around $60.

That gives HighPeak protection against further declines in oil prices, but otherwise its hedges have only slightly positive value at $60 oil.

Natural Gas 18,045,600 $2.50 $45
Gas Transportation and Processing -$70

I've modeled HighPeak's 2026 capex at $375 million, which is roughly 20% lower than its 2025 capex budget and assume that it can nearly maintain its oil production with that budget.

HighPeak is thus projected to generate $31 million in free cash flow in 2026 at $60 WTI oil. This would be around $11 million in free cash flow after paying its current $0.04 per share quarterly dividend.

HighPeak's $30 million quarterly term loan amortization payments are currently scheduled to resume in September 2026.

HighPeak's liquidity may thus start to decline in late 2026 based on my projections. It did increase its term loan borrowings to $1.2 billion recently, so it would be left with approximately $170 million in liquidity at the end of 2026 after factoring in $60 million in term loan repayments by the end of that year.

HighPeak should be fine if oil rebounds at least somewhat in 2027. However, in a continued $60 WTI oil environment, the $120 million in 2027 term loan repayments would then drain much of its remaining liquidity.

Thus in a continued $60 WTI oil environment, it may then need to take steps to improve its liquidity, such as further cutting capex, cutting its dividend, and/or doing an equity offering.

Estimated Valuation

I am trimming HighPeak's value slightly (by $0.25 per share) to $8.25 per share at long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil to reflect some slightly lower oil strip prices in late 2025 and 2026.

HighPeak should be okay in that scenario, but a lower for longer (than the end of 2026) scenario for oil prices would pressure its liquidity. Despite some upside in my base case scenario, I am remaining neutral on HighPeak's stock for now due to the risks around its debt.

Conclusion

Jack Hightower has retired from the company he founded and turned over the CEO position (at least on an interim basis to Michael Hollis). HighPeak's debt situation looks relatively challenging at $60 WTI oil as I believe that its free cash flow (after dividends) would be minimal if it wants to maintain oil production at that price.

It can probably handle that pricing scenario for a while, but once it is required to start making $30 million quarterly term loan repayments again (in September 2026), it could use $70+ WTI oil to keep its liquidity from dwindling.

data.title: NVDY: High Yields And NAV Supported By Nvidia's AI Investments
data.description:
If you are looking to tap into NVIDIA's (NASDAQ:NVDA) growth potential while benefiting from options-generated income, then the YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:NVDY) is an appropriate tool. The chart below shows that while its price performance (or NAV since it is a fund) has suffered from extreme volatility, its net inflows, despite fluctuating, have surged to $1.85 billion, demonstrating investor trust.

While highlighting how YieldMax limits potential gains during an upside and suffers from capital erosion risks during market downturns, this thesis aims to demonstrate that the option strategy is useful for navigating what could be a potential steady period of artificial intelligence growth. To support my point, I demonstrate how the semiconductor giant's investment in the AI ecosystem could support NVDY's NAV performance.

I start by comparing two key metrics - AUM ( assets under management) and yield, to highlight how it beats two other income-oriented ETFs based on NVIDIA.

NVDY's AUM is Rising the Fastest as it Pays the Highest Yield

Talking investor adoption, the increase of NVDY’s AUM to $1.85 billion over the last two years represents an average of roughly $77 million (1.85/24) of inflows per month since its inception in May 2023. This comes despite its significantly higher fees of 1.27%, as shown in the comparison table below, which demonstrates strong confidence in the fund’s income-generating strategy.

By comparison, the Roundhill NVDA WeeklyPay ETF (NVDW), which was launched in February this year, has garnered only $71 million to date, while the GraniteShares YieldBOOST NVDA ETF (NVYY) has managed $101 million in around six months.

Therefore, these two pale relative to NVDY, whose AUM growth primarily shows the appeal of its high-yield approach (78% as shown below), which primarily involves selling/writing call options on NVIDIA stock to generate monthly income. Now, the other two also use options strategies to generate income, but their approaches differ.

First, NVYY is designed to generate income primarily by selling put options on leveraged ETFs that track the 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF, rather than call options. This ETF seeks to deliver two times the daily performance of NVIDIA. In the same way as NVDY, it also sets limits on potential investment gains. As for NVDW, by employing a swap mechanism, it seeks to amplify returns by targeting 120% of the semiconductor giant's weekly performance. In this way, its tradable options strategy (including calls) offers uncapped and levered upside potential to the underlying asset (NVIDIA).

Noteworthily, this “uncapped” feature means no limit on gains as for the other two ETFs and largely explains why NVDW has largely outperformed its peers during the one, three, and six-month periods as tabled below.

However, the problem is that it uses leverage, which magnifies not only the gains but also the losses. Hence, since the semiconductor giant's stock gained around 72% during the last six months, NVDW managed to perform extremely well, but in case the stock suffers from volatility, things can be drastically different. Hence, an adverse move in the price of the underlying asset can cause a large NAV loss in the Roundhill ETF. This makes it more suitable for tactical positioning, like rapidly capitalizing on an expected short-term upside in Jensen Huang's company.

On the other hand, for those positioned for the longer term and ready to sacrifice some upside, YieldMax’s nearly 78% yield is the highest and makes sense, especially if the chipmaker sustains its upward trajectory, something possible thanks to its strategic investments and partnerships in the AI ecosystem.

NVIDIA's Upside Should Continue as it Enhances its Positioning in the AI Ecosystem

First, there is the $5 billion investment made in Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), a move that can help in better integrating its GPUs (Graphics Processing Units) with the latter's computing chips. This, in turn, enables NVIDIA to expand into the AI PC (computer) market, which is likely to grow at a phenomenal CAGR of 34.4% from 2025 to 2030, while at the same time improving its product positioning versus rivals like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) as competition in the AI space heats up.

Second, there is the $100 billion OpenAI partnership, which cements NVIDIA's role as a key infrastructure provider, given that the ChatGPT developer will use the money to finance the purchase of accelerated computing chips it needs to drive its software algorithms from the chipmaker itself. Thinking aloud, this direct partnership between an end customer (OpenAI) and NVIDIA, instead of renting GPUs in the traditional GPU-as-a-Service approach from cloud service providers like AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud, implies that the chipmaker is also becoming less dependent on the capital expenditure forecasts of hyperscalers.

Third, NVIDIA's new Blackwell platform offers a major performance leap (1.5x) over previous systems, and as the focus turns to reasoning and agentic AI, the demand for accelerated computing infrastructure is expected to continue in a Generative AI market forecasted to expand by 37.6% CAGR from 2025 to 2030.

In these circumstances, while it is unlikely for the semiconductor giant to repeat explosive revenue growth rates of above 100% like in fiscal 2025 in the near term, since its revenue base is larger, expectations for 2026 remain substantial, with both the topline and bottom line expected to grow by 58% and 51% respectively. This could increase as analysts adjust estimates to include the 10 GW of NVIDIA systems acquired by OpenAI to start going live, with the first phase (1 GW) as early as the second half of 2026.

Talking valuations, Wall Street's most bullish estimates an upside of 45%, but I believe this may be too high, as it is unlikely for the AI boom to continue with the same vigor. On the other hand, Wall Street's average price appreciation estimate of 15.7% seems more appropriate as it takes into account a more moderate scenario consisting of steady AI adoption. In such an event, given that NVDY has captured 12.5% (9% out of 72% as per the price performance charts) of NVIDIA upside during the last six months, it could appreciate by about 2% (15.7 x 12.5%).

This is not much, but, as its name itself suggests, YieldMax is primarily about deriving income out of volatility by selling covered calls on NVIDIA’s stock, which is then distributed as dividends. Hence, adding its 30.24% annualized yield (based on the 30-day yield of 2.52%) to the 2% appreciation, I obtain a total return of 32.24%, which explains my bullish position.

However, it is important to emphasize that a steadier adoption phase may not result in the dividends increasing, and there are also downside risks.

NVDY's Forward Path is Likely to be Moderate both for Income and NAV Performance

On the income front, we are far from NVIDIA’s massive upside in the first half of 2024, when volatility was also at a heightened level. This encouraged traders to pile into option calls. This resulted in higher premiums, translating into monthly distributions of above $2.5, as enclosed within the brown lines in the chart below. This also resulted in NVDY trending higher despite upside capping as the premium income largely compensated investors.

Things have now changed with distributions not exceeding $1 as shown above, with the NAV also staying below the $20 level. The reason is that NVIDIA is still gaining ground, but the degree of volatility is much lower, synonymous with a phase of more mature growth. This implies fewer option traders, which means lower premiums.

Therefore, going ahead, investors may become frustrated as they see NVIDIA rally steadily, but NVDY underperforms on income (lower yield) because of muted volatility and NAV appreciation due to the capped upside being applied on a stock that no longer pops. This may change if the AI investments rapidly translate into more sales than are expected, but one should be mindful of the execution risks involved in large and complex partnerships, which may take time to yield results.

Extending the discussion to risks, there may be no downside protection for NVDY's NAV if NVIDIA plunges. Such a plunge may occur in case of a DeepSeek-type AI breakthrough, as seen on January 27 this year, when NVIDIA’s stock lost 17% during a single session. The YieldMax fund was also down by that much.

YieldMax is not Immune to Downside Risks, but delivers

Now, taking into account Jensen Huang’s recent admission that China may be only one node (approximately 1 nm equivalent) behind America, his company could lose its pricing power as it faces more competition, in case the East Asian country closes the gap with the U.S. faster than expected. Therefore, YieldMax is not immune to capital losses.

Still, covered-call strategies like NVDY tend to dampen drawdowns versus the stock itself over a longer period. Hence, in the October 2024 to March 2025 period, when NVIDIA lost ground due to adverse market news, including the DeepSeek announcement (as highlighted in yellow in the above chart, option income continued to flow monthly, helping cushion losses.

In conclusion, this thesis has shown that for those wanting unleveraged exposure to NVIDIA's performance combined with dividends (as is the case with NVDW), NVDY provides better yields and NAV protection than NVYY. However, for growth investors used to the excitement provided by rallies, it is important to carefully weigh the tradeoff of putting money into NVDY versus holding NVIDIA stock directly. On the other hand, if you have an income-first mindset, are ready to accept upside limits, and prefer regular cash flow now instead of waiting for NVIDIA’s long-term capital appreciation, the ETF delivers.

data.title: Grifols: Significant Upside Likely Materializing
data.description:
I've been covering Grifols (NASDAQ:GRFS) for over 2 years. While I initially started out somewhat more dubious, what the company has done over the past few quarters, and over the last annual, reinforces my investment thesis from my previous article.

By that I mean that I believe it's going to be a very solid investment. I don't use wording like "massive upside" lightly - I only use it when I believe that there's a triple-digit, by which I mean over 150-200%, upside in the cards for an investment. In this case, I'm referring to the headline from my previous article, linked above, which I believe is materializing here.

Since my last article, Grifols has generated well over 24% RoR, including the company's dividend. Since this is less than 5 months, it's an impressive annualized upside, especially when you consider that the company may only have begun its recovery journey.

The foremost risk to Grifols and its thesis has, for some time, been leverage, which was over 6-7x when it was at its worst. But the company is, and has been successfully addressing this in its latest results, and the results are not bad at all.

In this article, I will therefore provide an update and show you why I am very happy that I invested in this company.

Grifols - Significant upside remains despite over 24% RoR in 5 months

I like finding companies with business models that "transcend" cycles. Companies that humans need, companies that our societies, in essence, depend on. This draws me to investments like Energy, utilities, food, real estate - but it can also go to companies like the recently covered Bureau Veritas, in certifications, or, as in this case, a company that works with blood plasma.

Having been around for going on 90 years now, the company Grifols is a leader in the field of plasma. It's Spanish-based, but with global operations. It collects, markets, and sells things like blood plasma, which is used for everything from diseases to just needing blood plasma overall.

One of the company's key market advantages is an established collection infrastructure, which it has established over the past 60+ years. The legal state of "paying" for blood/plasma donations varies greatly across the world, which means that the company focuses on countries and geographies where paying for this is legal.

This includes countries like the US, Central Europe, Austria, Hungary, Canada, and EMEA, like Egypt. With all of its collection centers, the company has the capacity to collect over 20 ML of blood plasma per year. That comes to about 5.3M gallons.

Aside from its core operations in blood plasma, the company is a pharma company focusing on R&D of products catering to the needs of labs and blood banks, a logical, synergistic operation to its plasma. It does this using subsidiaries or brands such as Talecris.

Overall, this company, as of 2Q25, holds a 20% market share of blood plasma collection in the world.

The company's valuation change should imply to you what the company managed in the latest results, 2Q25. The company managed 7% revenue growth, over 12.5% EBITDA growth, and over 20% at constant currencies, with a significant improvement in FCF that almost turned FCF positive.

Recall how I said the leverage was over 6-7x not that long ago?

Well, now leverage is down to 4.2x, which is a decline of 0.4x in less than 6 months from the full-year result. The company has also, as of this time, as mentioned above, reinstated the dividend. That was a full year before I expected this to happen, and even at a low level, this is a massive advantage.

Focus remains on solid FCF and deleveraging - but the impressive parts here is the EBITDA increase, the revenue increase, and the company's execution in what can only be characterized as a volatile macro.

Margin expansion is impressive here - the company is on track to deliver a double-digit volume growth on a per center basis, which should ratchet up that 20M L total to something higher.

Meanwhile, the company's biopharma operations are also "kicking off", with several indications either completed or on track. The advanced diagnostic segment is set to reinforce the company's existing, attractive market position and improve profitability, as well as reduce the through-cyclic volatility of the company.

From a 1H25 period perspective, the company managed record results. Momentum isn't stopping, but growing. Gross margins are close to the highest they've ever been, almost 40% at this time, with a 30 bps improvement YoY. EBITDA margin on an adjusted basis is up to over 25% for the 2Q25 period, which is to be compared to sub-20% levels when the company was in more trouble. All segments are contributing to this impressive level of results.

The company is furthermore on track to deliver even larger increases of FCF on a forward basis, and the lower leverage means that the company's capital allocation plans are very much on track.

The company has slightly changed its outlook for the FY25 period. The strong underlying trends currently in effect are expected to generate higher earnings, offset by the significant current weakness in the USD. The American currency has declined by over 10% this year. The company's EBITDA is partially benefiting from this; however, for every 0.01 USD depreciation against the EUR, the company is experiencing an EBITDA headwind of €7M - so the effect is significant. However, thanks to hedges and diversification, the impact can be said to be broadly neutral based on group profit, leverage, and company FCF.

The company has reaffirmed its improved guidance in terms of FCF, with a €425M higher-end FCF, before M&As, meaning it is forecasting a very significant improvement in free cash flow results for the full year before mergers and acquisitions planned by the company. This is a material improvement for FCF, which previously has been negative, and has been one of the larger arguments against the company's prospects and why it could be considered a "poor" investment.

The company's long-term advantages are related to its leading market position and improving company fundamentals. With these improvements, I don't see many risks that could impact the company in a big way negatively; rather, I expect the company to drive EPS up. Earnings are also expected to rise further going forward, and forecasts include expectations of around 25-30% per year, with an average annualized EPS growth of around 28% until 2027E. This is a very solid rate of growth, yet despite this, the company trades very significantly below its longer-term average.

And this is why I believe Grifols is undervalued here.

Let's look at the valuation for the company and what the upside here can be.

Valuation for Grifols - Upside is massive, I remain at "BUY", the dividend and improvements mean I'm raising my PT.

This is an encouraging article in terms of positive signs. I say that this company now represents a significant investment potential with a massive upside. I have been through the growth prospects in previous articles, but I believe it's time to reiterate them. Sometimes a picture says more than a thousand words, and the upside-down in this article is one such. This is the company's upside to a 5-year average.

The upside to that 5-year average here is 67%, or a 218% total potential RoR in 3 years. This implies a fair value of almost €39/share, and would in turn imply a PT of around €32/share. To remind you, my previous PT was €20/share, implying a significant discount to this, related to the previous lack of a dividend, as well as a higher leverage.

The logical result here, given improved results, is that it's time to increase both the fair value and the PT.

But to what?

The 20-year average is 26x P/E - I would go to a 20% discount to this, to about a 20x P/E, which comes to about €28/share in terms of fair value, allowing for an upside of over 40% per year, or 130% in total. The PT based on this increased fair value would come to €23/share, which for me is a 15% increase from my previous PT for Grifols.

Alternatives to Grifols still include Takeda, which also has an upside. Takeda has a 4.7% yield, is BBB+ rated, and is lower leveraged. However, at the same multiple, the potential RoR is much lower than with Grifols, and as such, I don't consider Takeda as attractive as Grifols.

Grifols remains my choice for investment here, with the following risks and considerations.

Risks & Considerations

The risks for this company are limited. The argument remains that Grifols has a relatively high leverage. While 4.2x is reduced, it's still relatively high compared to peers - and the company has work to still do here. However, I view the risk of this going off the rails as relatively low. Secondly, the previous lack of a dividend is now removed. While the dividend isn't massively compelling, it's an excellent and encouraging sign for investors, and surely one of the reasons the company is up. So that's a risk removed.

What remains as the most significant risk, as I see it, is that the company still faces competition from novel technologies and ways to replace traditional plasma that's collected from humans. If these methods become cost-effective, this could undermine the company's entire business model.

However, I will say that I have accounted and discounted for this in my valuation.

Thesis

Grifols remains the world leader in Plasma and plasma-related biopharma. This gives the company a clear advantage. However, overleverage has driven the credit rating to a B and canceled the company's relatively acceptable dividend, which has now been somewhat restored. This is now, after 2Q25, a better case, with Brookfield having dropped the company as a private buyout target, and the company's earnings prospects having materially improved.

I can call this non-yielding business a "Buy", no longer speculative as of this article, despite an upside in the double digits and an "easily" forecastable upside of over 20% per year until 2026E-2027E if the company's expectations materialize. As of the latest results, and 2025E, I believe we're closer than ever to materializing such an upside, with Q2 2025 confirming it even more than the previous one.

I'm keeping my target for the company as of quarterly results at a €20/share native PT, with a slightly different one for the ADR given the reflection of the preference instead of native share, which means that I'm positive, and own the stock, but I'm staying conservatively exposed to make up for the risk I see here. I might buy more at this time.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 2 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 5/5 "stars" here, which is enough for me to clear my hurdle and invest here. Based on this, and a price target of €23/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I maintain my "BUY" rating.

data.title: CGMM: Novel SMID ETF With A Racy Start Has Vulnerabilities
data.description:
Today, I would like to initiate coverage of the Capital Group U.S. Small and Mid Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:CGMM), with a Hold rating. I believe this rating is a nice place to start, as it perfectly reflects a balance between CGMM's advantages and vulnerabilities.

The fact is, despite being relatively novel, this vehicle already has something to boast about, including one of the strongest price returns in the subclass, a mostly high-quality portfolio with a growth tilt, and a massive growth in assets under management that have recently surpassed the $700 million milestone.

However, issues do exist.

  • First, I should emphasize that CGMM is not a pure-play classic SMID ETF (i.e., a portfolio with a weighted average market cap not exceeding $10 billion). It has exposure to mega caps, and its allocation to stocks with sub-$10 billion market caps is fairly modest.
  • Second, it has relatively easily beaten its peers, yet underperformed the market proxied with the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV). As my standards require a potential for outmaneuvering the market to qualify for a Buy rating, CGMM does not make the cut, especially considering it is quite an expensive product and has yet to prove itself.
  • Third, although I like its factor mix with a growth tilt, I would not say it is decisively ahead of the iShares Russell Mid-Cap ETF (IWR), as its quality is decent but not perfect.

Basics Of The Capital Group U.S. Small and Mid Cap ETF

  • Actively managed CGMM was incepted on January 14, 2025. Price data is available from January 16.
  • Capital Research and Management Company is the fund's adviser.
  • Expense Ratio: 0.51%
  • AUM: $723.58 million, as of October 2.
  • The ETF is rather liquid, with the 30-day median bid/ask spread at just 4 bps, as of October 1.

CGMM Strategy

From the Capital Group website, we know that CGMM is focused on capital appreciation, which it is supposed to achieve

... by investing in small and mid-capitalization companies, currently defined as companies whose market capitalizations fall within the range of the Russell 2500 Index or the Russell Midcap Index.

These two indices are tracked by the iShares Russell 2500 ETF (SMMD) and the iShares Russell Mid-Cap ETF, respectively, and we will be looking into the principal differences between CGMM and the portfolios of these vehicles below in the note.

It is also mentioned that the ETF relies on insights delivered by quite a sizable team of analysts:

The fund is managed as a core investment strategy and leverages more than 20 investment analysts contributing research to help portfolio managers identify attractive investment opportunities to pursue the fund’s objective.

CGMM Performance: A Racy Start, Yet Not Strong Enough

It will not be an exaggeration to say that CGMM has had a rather racy start, as it has confidently outperformed IWR and SMMD since January 16.

However, there is an issue here, and it is the fact that, at least for now, and this might change in the future, CGMM cannot capture enough of the market's upside during growth factor- and tech-driven rallies. Another way of saying, it has significantly underperformed IVV.

CGMM vs. Mid-Cap Blend ETFs

Nevertheless, CGMM is still a good product in its echelon. To corroborate, my calculations show that the median 6-month price return in the Mid-Cap Blend subclass is 12.1%. And CGMM has delivered a much stronger result: 18.01%.

Besides, there is a noticeable momentum in asset flows. More specifically, the median AUM in the subclass is about $182 million, while CGMM has already amassed $723.58 million, and it has less than 9 months in the books. Most ETFs in the group, 52 out of 90, have at least a five-year history.

What Does CGMM Do Better Than IWR?

As of September 29, there were 90 equities in the CGMM portfolio, with the cohort of the main ten having a 23.7% weight. So how different is this portfolio from those of the passively managed IWR and SMMD? Let us start with the overlap.

Parameter CGMM IWR
Weight of stocks present in both CGMM and IWR 80.3% 12.5%
Equity holdings 90 813

Calculated by the author using data from the ETFs

Parameter CGMM SMMD
Weight of stocks present in both CGMM and SMMD 51.83% 7.44%
Equity holdings 90 2,479

For SMMD, the aggregate underlying equity holdings are shown, including those of the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) that accounted for 41.48% of SMMD's net assets as of September 30

So we do see some significant similarities, which illustrate that CGMM mostly draws its ideas from the Russell Mid-Cap index universe. However, it is much more concentrated; hence, it is much pickier and quality-oriented.

Also, looking at the key ten holdings, we can already guess that the sector and factor allocations, and, likely, potential returns, are notably different in this group.

Sector Allocation

Looking at the sector exposures, we see that despite the considerable overlap, CGMM is in many aspects different from IWR and especially SMMD.

Sector CGMM (as of September 29) SMMD (as of September 30) IWR (as of September 30)

Created by the author using data from the ETFs. I marked Accelerant Holdings (ARX), one of CGMM's holdings, as 'Other' in the Sector column, as there were no sector classification data for it available as of October 2

  • Compared to IWR, the Capital Group ETF is overweight in the financials, consumer discretionary, consumer staples, and industrials sectors and underweight in communication, materials, real estate, energy, and utilities. Their exposures to IT are almost on par.
  • Compared to SMMD, CGMM is also overweight in these three sectors. It is meaningfully underweight in health care, materials, and real estate, among others.

Factor Mix: CGMM vs. IWR

As we discussed above, while IWR has exposure to 75 stocks that are in the CGMM portfolio, they account for just 12.5% of its net assets. So it is already intuitively evident that their factor stories have some unique characteristics. And a more important question is whether the Capital Group ETF has an edge here. So let us dig in to find out.

Size Factor

The paradox of SMID investing is that definitions of what exactly a small- or mid-cap company is vary vastly. There are classic definitions stating that a company should have a market cap from $300 million to $2 billion to qualify for a small-cap status and from $2 billion to $10 billion to be considered a mid cap. However, the reality appears more complex, as competing definitions do exist.

For example, as explained in the fact sheet from FTSE Russell available on the LSEG website:

The Russell Midcap Index is a subset of the Russell 1000® Index. It includes approximately 800 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership.

So what is the range of market caps in IWR's current portfolio? My analysis shows that as of September 30, it was from around $1.03 billion to $121.5 billion (yes, despite this looking quite strange, it did have exposure to mega caps thanks to Robinhood Markets (HOOD)). And its weighted average market cap was at around $30.84 billion.

And how significant is CGMM's exposure to stocks with less than $10 billion in market cap? Just a little more than a quarter of its net assets are allocated to them, while there are no small caps in this portfolio. This is much larger than IWR's allocation, though CGMM's WA market cap is still quite close to that of the iShares ETF.

Value Factor

As to the value factor, I would say that there is evidence supporting the point that CGMM's portfolio is more expensive than IWR's.

  • It is true that the adjusted weighted average EV/EBITDA shows CGMM is cheaper.
  • However, a different story is told by its adjusted WA earnings yield, which is significantly smaller.
  • It also has a higher Price/Sales ratio, which I have adjusted for IWR but not for CGMM, as the former has outliers with that multiple at over 100x.
  • CGMM has smaller exposure to stocks with at least a B- Quant Valuation grade.

Growth

Here, CGMM is clearly ahead of IWR, as illustrated by the WA forward growth rates and the Quant Growth grade data. And I believe this partly explains why it has outperformed the iShares Russell Mid-Cap ETF since inception: the growth factor is in vogue, so the stronger these characteristics, the better.

Quality

CGMM does not have an edge here, as all the selected quality indicators do not speak in its favor, even though IWR is still just a bit ahead.

However, in defense of CGMM, I should mention that it has considerably smaller exposure to free cash flow-negative companies.

Parameter CGMM IWR
FCF-negative companies 4.97% 10.58%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETF

Low Volatility

CGMM's holdings are just a bit more volatile, as illustrated by the weighted average 24-month and 60-month beta coefficients. Why does that matter? This gives it a slight advantage in the bull market, as higher beta means stronger upside capture.

Final Thoughts

So is CGMM a solid SMID ETF to buy into? It might be. We see some healthy momentum on the back of the broad market rally, also bolstered by some smart stock picking. With that being said, at least for now, I see no significant arguments for a Buy rating, especially considering it has underperformed IVV since inception. Nevertheless, nothing stands in the way of a conservative Hold.

data.title: Ondas Holdings: Autonomous Infrastructure In Place For Inflection, But Valuation Warrants Vigilance
data.description:
Ondas Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) brings about a high-risk, high-reward investment opportunity as the company is situated at the connection between autonomous robotics and industrial wireless infrastructure. Even though the company is still considered small cap, it is still poised for a plausible inflection year in 2025, supported by the latest $230 million capital fundraise, the recently attained defense contracts, as well as deepening cross-border alliances.

Capitalizing on regulatory impetus (including the vital Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) waivers), prominent smart city contracts in United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Tel Aviv, and a strategic rail digitization collaboration with Siemens Mobility, Ondas is actively constructing the foundation of the next-generation autonomous infrastructure. Even if only small-scale projects manage to scale, the business may quickly transition from a cash-burning model to a recurring-revenue platform with heightening gross margins.

Business Overview: A Vertically Integrated Autonomy Infrastructure Provider

Ondas is designedly structured into two key operational segments, which are brought together to form a vertically integrated autonomy infrastructure provider. As a result, the company is in the position to provide both the pivotal communication pillar in addition to the robust robotic systems to permit fully automated operations across mission-critical industries.

Ondas Autonomous Systems (OAS)

To begin with, the Ondas Autonomous Systems (OAS) business division was formed via the acquisitions of Airobotics Ltd. (Israel) and American Robotics, Inc. (United States). This merged entity creates and stations fully self-directed drone and ground robotic solutions designed for defense, smart cities, and industrial customers. All in all, the unit’s main value proposition would be its “drone-in-a-box” system, which allows entirely robot-driven, unmanned operations involving charging, launch, flight as well as landing.

In the meantime, OAS is equipped with numerous competitive advantages that are essential. Firstly, American Robotics has an extremely limited FAA waiver permitting Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS) operations in the United States (US). On top of that, Airobotics possesses active municipal contracts in high-growth markets such as the UAE and Tel Aviv, which comprises a partnership for the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA)-compliant combat drones in the US with Rift Dynamics alongside an initial $3.2 million defense contract for ground robotics payloads. Not to mention, the company now also has a newly established facility in Israel that caters for the scalable production of hardware to fuel the expanding defense industry vertical.

Ondas Networks

On the other hand, the Ondas Networks unit offers Software-Defined Radio (SDR) solutions through its flagship FullMAX platform. In essence, FullMAX facilitates private, crucial wireless networks. This, in turn, permits industrial clients — in particular, those from the energy, utilities, and railroad sectors — to deploy long-term evolution (LTE)-class communications over licensed spectrum in which public networks may be compromised or undependable.

An important stimulus for this division would be its association with strategic partner Siemens Mobility, whereby strategic partner, Siemens Mobility, is integrating its FullMAX SDR system into Ondas Networks’ US rail control system product to streamline Class I railroad communications. Upon the triumphant execution of pilot projects, these integrations are anticipated to shift into the wider commercial deployments commencing late 2025.

Core Growth Drivers: 2025 Catalysts

Ultimately, the bullish thesis for Ondas depends entirely on the company’s capability in realizing and scaling three central opportunities. These drivers would subsequently imply that the business is finally progressing from a prototype in its research and development (R&D) stage into a multi-vertical commercial platform — resulting in 2025 becoming Ondas’ vital inflection point.

The Long-Term Growth Opportunity Lying In Its Rail Digitization Through Siemens Mobility

Quintessentially, Ondas’ collaboration with Siemens Mobility is targeting the enormous, multi-billion dollar modernization of the Class I railroad communications infrastructure in the US. The FullMAX SDR system by Siemens Mobility is pivotal in the provision of a steady, exclusive wireless network that is necessary for mission-critical signaling and control applications.

Meanwhile, early-stage deployments — such as the upgrading of Chicago's metropolitan commuter rail, Metra — are focusing on the replacement of the legacy Positive Train Control (PTC) systems. These initiatives are also expedited by federal safety mandates in combination with the strong advocacy for digitised control networks.

Now, with Siemens Mobility aggressively marketing its FullMAX SDR system towards its US-based railway stakeholders, early commercial deployment are forecasted to kickstart towards the end of 2025, thus moving Ondas Networks from its pilot phase to a promising division of continuous infrastructure revenue.

Growing the Autonomous Drone Revenue Stream via Smart Cities and Worldwide Deployments

Interestingly, Ondas is the first-mover within the city-scale autonomous drone infrastructure sector. In fact, the company has been actively stationing drone stations for urban surveillance and public safety purposes upon signing a five-year agreement with Tel Aviv back in 2024. This project, in conjunction with present-day operations in the UAE, has started to bring about revenue-generating contracts in Q2 2025.

Given that the company’s drone-in-a-box systems are offered in the form of long-standing Infrastructure-as-a-Service agreements, the business division’s growth rate would be generating a vital recurring top-line base as more cities begin to adopt aerial reconnaissance solutions. Consequently, this vertical is a primary driver behind Ondas’ reiterated 2025 revenue target of $25 million.

Diversification Across the Manufacturing and Defense Sectors

Ondas has achieved substantial diversification of its operations into the ground robotics as well as defense sectors in 2025:

Ground Robotics: Ensuing the acquisition of Apeiro Motion Ltd in August 2025, the company has effectively brought in a $3.5 million defense deal for Apeiro-branded Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs) and mission payloads. This thereby officiates Ondas’ entry into the rugged ground robotics market.

Combat Drones: Ondas’ strategic investment into the Norwegian defense technology firm, Rift Dynamics, resulted in the business securing exclusive US distribution rights for the NDAA-compliant WÅsp combat drone platform. With that, the earliest batch of 500 units are already in place for initial delivery starting in October 2025.

Large-Scale Manufacturing: These novel defense verticals are underpinned by Ondas’ manufacturing facility in Israel, which went into full operation in late 2024. Essentially, this facility is paramount for the company to build a production line that is scalable and margin-friendly across all ground-based and aerial robotics.

Latest Financial Snapshot

Excitingly, Ondas has declared its strongest quarterly performance to date in Q2 2025. In particular, the business has roped in a total revenue of $6.27 million, representing a magnificent year-over-year (YoY) increment of 555%.

Most importantly, the Q2 period also recorded an impactful step-change in the business's profitability standpoint. Specifically, gross profit hit the $3.33 million mark and translated to a gross margin of 52.7%, which is a huge improvement in contrast to the gross loss reported in Q2 2024. This accelerated margin widening can be greatly attributed to a more favorable revenue mix that is fueled mainly by the long-term federal and municipal Infrastructure-as-a-Service drone deployment contracts.

As investors glance into the future, the management has restated its full-year FY2025 top-line guidance of $25 million, citing enhanced visibility and solid execution capabilities as primary drivers. This conviction is ultimately reinforced by the record-setting backlog of $22 million, which props up the revenue conversion into Q3 and Q4 2025.

Even though the documented operating costs of $12.6 million has led to an operating loss of $9.2 million, this activity is consistent with a business that is prioritising aggressive upscaling. That said, the leadership has effectively derisked the company’s image via the successful $230 million equity raise back in September 2025. Overall, this cash infusion supplies the firm with an extended 12- to 18-month operational runway so that the management can concentrate intently on their backlog conversion, execution, and production scaling without having to worry about finances in the near-term.

Valuation Analysis: It is a Hold For Now

In short, the valuation for Ondas may be considered to be extremely stretched.

Based on the data as at September 2025, the company’s stock has been substantially outpacing its industry peers, trading at a trailing twelve month (TTM) Enterprise Value to Sales (EV/Sales) multiple of 146.77×:

While Ondas’ unique standing in the autonomous infrastructure segment alongside its thrilling growth prospects validates a premium, this extensive gap between the firm and its peers signifies considerable optimism and leaves virtually negligible room for any mistakes.

Should we apply a still-generous TTM EV/Sales multiple of 20× to Ondas’ $16.1 million TTM revenue, the proposed enterprise value (EV) would thereby be around $322 million. With reference to the present stock count of 101.5 million, this would lead us to $3.17 as the fair share price. And based on the current share price of $7.41, this presents a 57% downside risk as a possibility.

In other words, this means that the market has already priced in the business’s extraordinary growth across all industry verticals simultaneously. Hence, any slowdowns in project execution and/or behind schedule revenue ramp-ups is most likely going to spark a significant re-rating.

As a result, in spite of my hope in the company’s long-term growth story, Ondas’ present-day valuation is far too stretched to warrant a bullish stance. Thus, I have decided to assign a Hold rating for the time being.

Key Investment Risks to Take Note Of

Besides that, although Ondas presents plenty of room for growth, investors need to understand that the business still faces numerous regulatory, operational, and financial challenges. Considering that the company’s heightened valuation leaves zero margin for error, this thereby justifies the careful monitoring of acute risks that may derail the growth story.

Risks Associated with Execution and Revenue Increment

Although the company has a sturdy client pipeline, it remains important to acknowledge that Ondas is still pivoting from the running of pilot programmes to full-scale, recurring contracts. Hence, any hindrance in translating the backlogs from Siemens Mobility, Tel Aviv, or the US defense sector may expose the share price to sharp corrections due to the firm’s ongoing cash burn and elevated fixed costs.

In essence, investors are basically making weighty bets on Ondas’ flawless, simultaneous top-line increments across all of its sector verticals.

Capital Intensity and Dilution

Ondas business operations, including constructing drone stations, stationing radio networks, and developing robotics, is highly capital-intensive. Even though the newly raised $230 million keeps the firm buoyant for the time-being, Ondas is still vulnerable to the likelihood of having to raise additional capital if its revenue growth rate remains insufficient to offset the operational expenses.

Moreover, the future equity raises bear the risk of significantly eroding shareholder returns as there are already 101.5 million shares outstanding (which already marks a notable YoY rise from 69.9 million shares outstanding).

Reliance on Public Policies and Regulations

A noteworthy portion of the company’s growth prospects remain sensitive to government procedures. This comprises matters such as the critical FAA waivers for BVLOS operations, municipal contracts, as well as the procurement cycles for the defense sector.

Consequently, any amendments to the FAA policy, realignments to the defense budget, or volatility in the political environment of key markets may adversely impact the deployment rate, leading to potential delays or even cancellations. So, this heavy dependence on a focused group of public sector customers makes the company’s top line relatively less foreseeable as opposed to private technology players.

The Bottom Line

All in all, Ondas is in an excellent position to capitalise the high growth potential of its niches — whether it is regarding artificial intelligence or autonomous robotics — as part of securing wireless networks for critical infrastructure. Furthermore, the municipal deployments, defense sector contract victories, and strong ties with Siemens Mobility all imply a business foundation that have been meticulously crafted for a major revenue inflection.

Still, it is evident that investor expectations have run ahead of fundamentals. Taking into account that Ondas’ shares have been trading at approximately 150× revenue multiple and that the journey to achieving consistent profitability is heavily contingent on future executions, the stock could be deemed aggressively priced. Hence, with all these risks and the relatively high chance of a notable downward correction if Ondas’ growth falters, I maintain my stance to remain cautious and assign the company a Hold rating.

Moving forward, I will continue with inspecting the management’s execution of Ondas’ Tel Aviv scaling project, the rail deployment partnership with Siemens Mobility, in addition to the efficacy of the company’s defense backlog conversion. Ultimately, I will need concrete proof of the company’s high-margin and sustainable top line improvement before I am able to justify a re-evaluation of my current rating.

data.title: ISM Manufacturing PMI: Slower Contraction In September
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Institute for Supply Management (ISM) manufacturing purchasing managers' index (PMI) came in at 49.1 in September, indicating contraction in U.S. manufacturing for a seventh straight month. The latest reading marks a slightly slower rate of contraction than in August and was higher than the forecast of 49.0.

Here is an excerpt from the latest report:

Spence continues, “In September, U.S. manufacturing activity contracted at a slightly slower rate, with production growth the biggest factor in the 0.4-percentage point gain of the Manufacturing PMI®. However, the combined drops in the New Orders and Inventories indexes (4.2 percentage points) exceeded the increase in the Production Index (3.2), rendering the Manufacturing PMI® improvement negligible. Last month’s increase in new orders (an index gain of 4.3 percentage points from July to August) seems to have flowed through to production but does not appear to be sustainable given the subsequent drop in new orders in September.

“One of the four demand indicators improved, with the Backlog of Orders Index showing a gain of 1.5 percentage points (which could be due to August’s increase in new orders, cited above), while the New Orders, New Export Orders and Customers’ Inventories indexes contracted at faster rates. A ‘too low’ status for the Customers’ Inventories Index is usually considered positive for future production.

“Regarding output, the Production and Employment indexes improved, though 64 percent of panelists’ comments still indicated that managing head count is still the norm at their companies, as opposed to hiring.

“Finally, inputs (defined as supplier deliveries, inventories, prices and imports), on net, moved further into contraction territory. The Supplier Deliveries Index indicated slower deliveries, the Inventories Index worsened, and the Prices Index continued to increase, but at a slower rate. The Imports Index moved further into contraction.

“Looking at the manufacturing economy, 67 percent of the sector’s gross domestic product (GDP) contracted in September, down from 69 percent in August. Twenty-eight percent of GDP is strongly contracting (registering a composite PMI® of 45 percent or lower), up from 4 percent in August. The share of sector GDP with a PMI® at or below 45 percent is a good metric to gauge overall manufacturing weakness. Of the six largest manufacturing industries, only one (Petroleum & Coal Products) expanded in September, compared to two in August,” says Spence.

Background on ISM Manufacturing PMI

The ISM Manufacturing Index aims to measure the health of the manufacturing sector through a survey. The ISM Manufacturing Index is a diffusion index, meaning that a reading above 50 indicates expansion in the sector compared to the previous month and a reading below 50 indicates contraction. Additionally, the distance from 50 indicates the extent of expansion or decline.

Here is the table of PMI components.

ISM Manufacturing PMI and Recessions

What sort of correlation does the ISM Manufacturing PMI have with recessions? The month before a recession, the index has ranged between 42.1 and 66.2, with an average of 49.7, a level we currently sit below.

As you can see, the index's value at the start of recessions has had quite a range, some in expansion territory and some in contraction territory. Therefore, it is more useful to look at the behavior of the PMI during those recessions (always contracting) and the behavior of the PMI outside of recessions (mostly expanding).

Here is a closer look at the series beginning at the turn of the century. The latest reading keeps the index in contraction territory for a sixth straight month after two months in expansion. Prior to that, the index contracted for 26 consecutive months from November 2022 to December 2024, the longest streak on record.

ISM PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by the Institute for Supply Management. See the latest update on the Services PMI here.

ETFs associated with industrials and manufacturing include the First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX Fund (FXR), Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI), Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS), and iShares U.S. Industrials ETF (IYJ).

data.title: Nonresidential Construction Forecast 2025-27: Tapering Down
data.description:
The nonresidential construction sector of the economy has changed substantially in recent years. Contractors in this sector and their suppliers must understand how fabs and data centers have altered the long-time concentrations of the different components of total activity. And public sector contractors should understand the winding down of the 2021 infrastructure bill.

Businesses leaders should expect continued growth of data centers but a retrenchment among semiconductor fabs, with small declines across most other parts of the industry.

Chip Fab Construction

Semiconductor fabs and data centers dominated the growth of nonresidential construction since 2022, as shown in the chart nearby. Chip fabs, though, are tapering off. (The data actually cover all facilities manufacturing computers and electronics, but chip fabs drive the changes.) The surge began with the post-pandemic chip shortage. Automakers had dialed down their chip orders in anticipation of weak sales. When stimulus money pushed up demand for cars, the chip makers had already dedicated their production capacity to other uses. Chip prices soared and new fabs were built as quickly as possible - which is never very quickly. Afterwards, the Biden administration pushed the CHIPS Act, which added federal funding for fabs.

The semiconductor industry, though, has long been to be susceptible to boom-bust cycles. The upfront capital costs are extremely high, while the ongoing production costs are relatively low. Once a fab is built, equipped and the personnel trained, operation continues even if prices drop a good deal. The continued supply into a soft market further depresses prices. Little new capacity is added. Then demand eventually increases, and prices soar because it takes a long time to get new fabs up and running. This cycle is common to many capital-intensive sectors, including mining, airlines and shipping.

Data Center Construction

Although fab construction has peaked, data center construction continues to grow. The earlier wave was driven by cloud storage. Now artificial intelligence needs more data center capability. The first needs for AI were model development: building the AI systems. That continues, but interpretation - using the models to answer questions - requires more data centers. Although the course of AI usage is not easily forecasted, increasing application of the technology is the obvious bet.

Other Private Nonresidential Construction

Some of the other parts of private nonresidential construction were impacted by the pandemic and its aftermath. Warehouse construction rose rapidly as people switched to online shopping, and retail construction dropped. As demand for warehouse space increased, developers saw the opportunity and … overbuilt! It's not a terribly bad sector in most cities, but certainly overbuilt. And retail probably underbuilt. Some of the old shopping malls are now obsolete, but the retail market is enjoying more demand for activities, such as gyms, yoga studios, hands-on pottery shops, etc. Careful development will prove profitable.

Office space was found to be overbuilt when people began working from home in large numbers. Even the brightest markets have above-normal vacancy today, but there are always some unmet needs. Suburban medical offices, for example, are growing.

Hotel/motel demand increased after the pandemic lockdowns were over, leading to rising construction in 2022. Now that sector has adequate capacity, and only small increases will be likely in the coming years. Manufacturing construction not counting chip fabs has declined over the past year and probably has sufficient capacity in most cases.

Public Construction

Public sector construction enjoyed a big boost with the 2021 infrastructure bill, which appropriated $1.2 trillion, more than twice the usual public construction budget. Spending will continue for a couple more years, as many of the projects entail multi-year activity. But the ramp-up phase is over. A little bit more plateau will ensue, and then construction will decline with the end of the huge federal funding. Infrastructure is not nearly as large a priority for Congress as it was a few years back.

Construction Forecast

Total nonresidential construction will taper down. Slower growth in the overall economy dictates lower need for new buildings. Tariffs and limited labor force growth have increased costs, which will prevent marginal ideas from becoming active projects. The sector will not crash but will gradual decline over the next two years.
